title: Gigot d'Agneau
subtitle: French roast leg of lamb
excerpt: A leg of lamb rubbed with Dijon mustard, garlic, and rosemary, then roasted until the surface is caramelised and the interior stays pink. The French approach to a Sunday joint.
sourceNotes: Gigot d'agneau rôti is one of the central dishes of the French Sunday table, particularly associated with Easter. Documented in Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, and Simone Beck, Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) and Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960).

content.text: The French version of roast leg of lamb differs from the Greek (page ref) in its simplicity and its endpoint: a French gigot is cooked pink, rested properly, and sliced at the table. The Dijon mustard in the rub forms a crust that seals the surface and intensifies during roasting.

At 200°C fan with a 20-minute rest, this gives lamb that is pink throughout for a 2 kg bone-in leg. If the leg is larger, add 10 minutes per extra 500 g and use a thermometer: 60°C at the thickest point is medium-rare; 68°C is medium.

items.name: Garlic
items.prepNote: half slivered for studding, half crushed for the rub

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Cut 20 deep slits across the lamb with a thin sharp knife. Push a garlic sliver and a few rosemary leaves into each slit. Mix the crushed garlic, mustard, butter, remaining rosemary and thyme, salt, and pepper into a paste. Rub all over the lamb.

Place the lamb in the roasting pan. Roast at 200°C for 15 minutes until the surface begins to colour, then reduce to 180°C and continue for a further 50 to 60 minutes (70 to 75 minutes total for medium-rare on a 2 kg leg). Transfer to a board, cover loosely with foil, and rest for 20 minutes. Carve and serve with gratin dauphinois and green beans.

items.fix: Reduce the temperature after 15 minutes; if it's darkening too fast, tent loosely with foil
items.cause: Oven too hot throughout; the initial blast should be brief

items.fix: Use a thermometer, 60°C is medium-rare; also rest the lamb fully, as the temperature rises 3 to 5°C during resting
items.cause: Cooked too long or not rested

content.text: Gigot d'agneau rôti is the Easter and spring Sunday dish in France. Lambs slaughtered in spring when they are milk-fed give the best quality (agneau de lait or agneau de Pauillac). In Brittany the lamb from the salt marshes (pré-salé) has a mineral flavour from the plants on which it grazes; in Provence it is paired with garlic, thyme, and olive oil rather than mustard.
